seo.title: Building AI Coding Assistant with BeeAI + Vex Guardrails
seo.description: Learn how to build ai coding assistant with BeeAI + Vex guardrails. Detect failures, prevent drift, and ensure reliability.

content.intro: Building an AI Coding Assistant with BeeAI enables powerful code-aware agents that analyze repositories, execute in sandboxes, validate tests, and debug iteratively. However, long coding sessions risk unbounded memory growth, tool injection vulnerabilities, state serialization failures, and inconsistent model outputs from Granite. Vex provides essential guardrails to ensure reliability, security, and correctness in production deployments.

architectureOverview.heading: AI Coding Assistant Architecture with BeeAI
architectureOverview.description:
The AI Coding Assistant uses BeeAI's agent framework with a Granite LLM backbone, integrating repository context via git tooling, sandboxed code execution through isolated Python environments, automated test validation with pytest integration, and iterative debugging loops. Agents maintain conversation memory across sessions while tools handle file I/O, code generation, and vulnerability scanning. Observability is built-in via BeeAI's event system, making Vex integration seamless through event listeners.

Key components include: a central CodeAgent with memory store, ToolRegistry for repo analysis (git diff, ast parsing), execution sandbox, and verification suite (bandit for security, safety checks, license scanners). The agent loop processes user requests like 'fix this bug' by retrieving context, generating code, testing, and iterating until validation passes.

BeeAI's event-driven architecture emits lifecycle events (agent_start, tool_call, memory_update, response_generated) that Vex monitors for proactive intervention.

risks.name: Unbounded Memory Growth
risks.description: Long debugging sessions accumulate massive context windows with code diffs and execution logs
risks.mitigation: Vex monitors memory events and triggers summarization/expiration policies

risks.name: Tool Injection via Code Execution
risks.description: Malicious user prompts craft payloads exploiting sandbox escape or command injection
risks.mitigation: Vex validates tool inputs with AST parsing and syscall whitelisting before execution

risks.name: Credential Leakage in Codebase
risks.description: Agents process repos containing API keys, tokens, or secrets in generated fixes
risks.mitigation: Vex scans memory and outputs with regex patterns and entropy detection

risks.name: Insecure Dependency Introduction
risks.description: Agent-generated requirements.txt includes vulnerable or malicious packages
risks.mitigation: Vex runs safety checks and license validation on all dependency changes

implementation.title: BeeAI CodeAgent with Repo Context
implementation.description: Initialize the core agent with git tooling and memory for repository-aware coding
implementation.code:
from beeai import Agent, Tool
from beeai.tools import GitTool, PythonREPL

repo_tool = GitTool(repo_path='/workspace/project')
exec_tool = PythonREPL(sandbox=True, timeout=30)

coding_agent = Agent(
    name='CodeAssistant',
    model='ibm/granite-20b',
    memory_size=100,
    tools=[repo_tool, exec_tool],
    system_prompt="You are a secure coding assistant. Analyze repos, fix bugs, validate tests. Never execute untrusted code."
)

@vex.listener('agent_start')
def validate_session(event):
    if len(event['memory']) > 50:
        event['memory'] = summarize_context(event['memory'])

vexIntegration.title: Register Vex Event Listener
vexIntegration.description: Hook Vex into BeeAI's event system to monitor all agent activity
vexIntegration.code:
from vex import Vex
from beeai import Agent
from vex.integrations.beeai import BeeAIListener

# Initialize Vex client
vex = Vex(api_key='vex-your-key')

# Create BeeAI listener
listener = BeeAIListener(vex_client=vex)

# Register on agent events
coding_agent.add_event_listener(listener)

# Define custom verifications
@vex.verification('code_correctness')
def verify_code(event):
    code = event['tool_call']['code']
    exec_result = sandbox_exec(code)
    return {
        'passed': exec_result.success,
        'test_coverage': exec_result.coverage,
        'evidence': exec_result.stdout
    }
